data.title: NFLY: Great Option To Boost Profits On Netflix
data.description:
Introduction

The YieldMax NFLX Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NFLY) is a fund that seeks to provide consistent income, on a monthly basis, by selling and writing calls on the underlying security, Netflix, Inc. (NFLX). This fund tries to provide investors with a consistent yield, while retaining capped upside on gains for NFLX. This ETF’s main objective is income, and its secondary objective is to benefit from share appreciation in NFLX. Below are some key facts from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.99%.

  • AUM: $169.5M.

  • Volume: 413K.

Its top holdings are shown in the image below - as you can see, cash in the form of US Treasuries and then various derivative exposures on NFLX.

In the section below, we will review recent relevant news and headlines for NFLX and see if investors should be open to gaining exposure to NFLX through this ETF.

Recent News & Trends

Earnings

NFLX recently reported earnings, and despite a beat on both top and bottom lines, the shares slipped following the announcement. To sum up the highlights: EPS came in at $7.19 versus $7.08 consensus, and revenue was $11.08B versus $11.07B estimates. Overall revenue grew 16% compared to the previous quarter, and the firm updated its full-year guidance to be between $44.8B and $45.2B, up from a range of $43.5B to $44.5B. These all look fairly solid upfront. Again, for the second quarter in a row, NFLX has determined it would be in their best interest not to release an update on subscription data, which leaves something to be desired, but it looks like the firm has been able to grow nicely this past quarter. Furthermore, net cash from operations also increased, which is always something that we like to see as it shows how well a company’s core business is doing at producing cash, and it looks like NFLX did very well here. Their $2.4B cash generated from operations was up 84% from the prior-year period, and free cash flow guidance was also increased to $8-$8.5B. All of these look strong upfront, and we believe that this is mainly driven by 2 factors: 1) their continued clamp down on sharing accounts, which makes more individuals purchase their own subscription, and 2) their ongoing investment into original content creation. Shows that are in the pipeline, such as Stranger Things, have a devout fanbase, and NFLX has only stirred the interest more for their upcoming next season with their trailer that was recently released. We view their slight downturn since earnings as a possible catalyst to enter a position in the company, as fundamentally speaking, their reports look healthy.

Use of AI in their content creation

NFLX has been reported to use AI in creating content, and we think that despite being a bit of a controversial issue in filmmaking, this can be good from an investor’s perspective. As AI is used to generate backgrounds and improve special effects, the overall cost of production should in theory decrease over time as the AI models are trained to be better and better over time at enhancing video quality and output. We do not expect nor believe that a majority of a video production will be AI-generated (we hope that this is not the case) but we certainly believe that there can be time/effort, and therefore cost, savings generated from using AI to touch up raw video footage. Per the CEO, Ted Sarandos, there were efficiencies of up to 10x faster production by using AI to enhance visual effects and optimize workflows. In the future, viewers may find it difficult to determine what has been AI-generated and what has not been, but from an investment standpoint, it may soon be undeniable how significant the cost savings and productivity enhancements AI can generate for the company. This represents a positive benefit for the company in the future.

Market share remains strong and their original content is a powerhouse

Despite the growth in streaming competitors, the data shows that NFLX has taken top positioning amongst its peers in the US video streaming on demand market for original content in movies. See the graph below that highlights the top players.

NFLX is clearly beating even Disney+ which has a massive trove of original content under the Disney umbrella.

NFLX is also in the lead when it comes to original content, TV shows as well. Despite other players catching up a bit in this segment, their lead is still definitive - see below.

We believe that NFLX will continue to do well in original content, and per a report, the firm is set to spend $18B in developing original content this year alone. This figure is also set to rise, as the CFO states in the report. We view this as very bullish for NFLX shares in the long run and feel that with their lead in market share, continuing to create great content will only push them further. This is another positive sign for investors looking to gain exposure to NFLX.

Covered Call Strategy

NFLY’s strategy is to own NFLX stock (either directly or synthetically via options) and then buy/sell calls to generate income. The fund will employ one of two strategies at any given time: selling covered calls or selling a covered call spread. We will explore each of these strategies below.

Covered Calls

The covered call strategy involves owning shares of a stock and then selling call options against that holding at a strike price that is higher than the current price. Let’s use the following scenario to explore this strategy:

  • We buy 100 shares of NFLX at $100 a share.

  • We sell a NFLX call option at a strike price of $110 for a premium of $300.

The chart below shows your potential returns depending on the price of NFLX.

The blue line represents your returns if you did not sell the option and the red line represents your returns if you did sell the option. We see that the return with options increases with NFLX’s share price, but after it hits $110 (the strike price of the option), returns are fixed and do not increase. On the flipside, the options strategy is always higher than the strategy without options when looking at any price below $113. This is because we gained $300 by selling the option.

Overall, this strategy is good if NFLX increases a little, but not beyond $110. If NFLX decreases in value, your losses are lower because you made money on selling the call option.

Now let’s look at the covered call spread scenario. A covered call spread is one in which an investor sells a call option and then buys a call option at a higher strike price. NFLY “writes” covered call spreads, meaning that they actually do the opposite - they buy a call option and then sell a call option at a higher strike price.

Scenario:

  • Buy 100 shares of NFLX at $100 per share.

  • Buy a $110 strike call option for $300.

  • Sell a $120 strike call option for $150.

In this scenario, if NFLX goes up by enough, then you will make more money than just buying the equity. However, if NFLX decreases in value or does not increase enough, you will be worse off by selling the options. The amount by which you are worse off in this scenario is $150 because you spent $300 to buy one call and made $150 to sell the other for a total expenditure of $150. This is a good strategy to employ if you are expecting NFLX to increase significantly and want to boost your gains.

Overall, each strategy is best in different scenarios:

  • Selling Covered calls: When you expect NFLX to increase mildly or possibly decrease.

  • Selling a covered call spread: When you expect NFLX to increase significantly.

Risks

There are many risks when it comes to buying NFLY, and they are all outlined in detail in the summary prospectus including risks related to derivatives, and using synthetic positions. One risk we want to highlight is the fund’s management. We explored the 2 types of strategies the fund uses, but in order to make a profit from these strategies you need to know 1) which one to use at a given period of time and 2) which calls to buy and/or sell. Both of these decisions are dependent on a good prediction of how the price of NFLX will move. The good news is that as long as NFLX increases in value you will always either make a profit or suffer a relatively small loss. However, there is also the consideration of opportunity cost. In the covered call scenario we discussed, if NFLX increases to $120, you would make $1,300 in profit. But if you had not sold any calls you would make $2,000 in profit meaning that you left $700 on the table. Overall, the managers of NFLY have a difficult job of predicting NFLX’s price movement and making the correct decisions and it is a very real risk that they may not.

Conclusion

We are very bullish on NFLX as it has strong earnings growth, clever AI capabilities to reduce costs, and is focusing investments on original content. The two options strategies the fund utilizes can help boost income from NFLX especially if you are bullish on NFLX’s overall prospects. There is, of course, the risk of the options strategy actually resulting in lower profits, but considering the most recent distribution rate of 32.71%, we see that the fund so far has more than delivered.

data.title: 7 Quarters Of Growth: What's Fueling The Rally In Preferred Shares?
data.description:
Preferred shares have seen seven consecutive quarters of gains, but can that move higher continue? James Hunter, VP, Director and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management, gives his view.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Preferred shares have seen seven consecutive quarters of gains, but can that run continue or are things looking a bit stretched? Joining us now to discuss is James Hunter, VP, Director and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management. Great to have you back on the program.

James Hunter: Thank you, Greg. We've got our summer ties on.

Greg Bonnell: We've got our summer ties. We're looking nice and airy and bright. We're going to keep the conversation airy and bright. But preferred shares-- seven consecutive quarters-- that is quite the run. Still a healthy risk reward out there in the space, or are we getting a little stretched?

James Hunter: Yeah, I mean it's been awesome. We're up about 10% year-to-date. That follows last year-- I think we're up 20%, 25% on the year. So it's been a continuation of a nice recovery in preferred shares.

What's really going on is a scarcity effect. You're continuing to see a lot of redemptions in the market. The banks have been very active in redeeming some of their preferred shares at par.

But it's not just the banks. It's also the insurers and the utility companies. Even in the energy space, we had $6 billion in redemptions last year, $2 billion so far this year, another billion to come. So like 20% of the market in a bit of a hurry in the last year or two.

So, yeah, it's been pushing prices higher. And at this point, I'd say the risk-reward is balanced. You're getting a good 5% yield. Prices could go higher because of these redemptions that are ongoing in the market. But on the other hand, something unexpected could happen that could take the market a little lower. So, we're sort of neutral on the asset class at the moment.

Greg Bonnell: I know "unexpected" by its very nature is hard to define, but what could be a catalyst that could take the market lower? What kind of things usually could hit preferreds?

James Hunter: Yeah, so it would be something to do with the economy that would drive central banks to maybe cut rates, or for the bond market to price interest rates lower. And we saw that in April with the-- we called it the tariff tantrum, with Liberation Day and what followed thereafter. I mean, the market was really worried that we were going to move quickly towards a recession.

And that's an environment that's not good for preferred shares, partly because issuers would be making less money in a recession, and also because, as interest rates come down, that really affects the perspective outlook for dividend payments on rate reset preferred shares.

So that's the thing that we're always looking for. By its nature, it can be hard to predict. And so that's why it's important to be disciplined about the pricing that you're paying for preferreds.

Greg Bonnell: Those are the possibilities, the reality we have been living through with those seven consecutive quarters of gains. You mentioned that some prefs are approaching par. What's the significance of that for someone who's not sure about prefs so far?

James Hunter: So, important to remember that preferred shares, when they're issued, they come to market at $25 per share here in Canada. And what we've seen since this asset class really became much bigger in the last 10-15 years, they average around $21 a share. So, sort of like a 15% discount to par. And that's because sometimes they trade at $25, and sometimes they trade at $17, $18 a share. It could be a 25% or 30% discount when we go through those periods of uncertainty.

And we've seen that before. In 2015, when oil prices collapsed, they didn't go to zero at that time, but they went a lot lower. That was a drag on the Canadian economy. We saw it in 2020 in the pandemic, and then in 2022-2023 with all those rate hikes and fears around recession. So during those periods, preferred shares can trade a lot lower than their par value.

So it's important to be aware of where you are in that cycle. Right now, we're overshooting a little bit the long-term averages. That's OK, because it's supported by fundamentals around redemptions and corporate profitability. So we're not worried about it. But on the other hand, we're just cognizant that the risk-reward isn't as appealing.

Greg Bonnell: Yeah. We've had interesting conversations on the program about the equity side of this. No shortage of worries on the economy. Equity markets don't seem to be worried. When you talk about these redemptions, what's happening here in the redemption space?

James Hunter: OK, so when we talk about redemptions, the primary driver here is a substitution away from preferred shares towards other types of capital for primarily the banks, but also the regulated insurance companies. They are calling back their preferred shares, and they're moving to something called limited recourse capital notes. The interest payments they make on those securities are tax-deductible for them, so it's a lower cost of capital, lower cost of financing.

There are also, in other parts of the market, the pipelines, the utilities-- they have the ability to offer hybrid securities. Also, it's like an alternative to a typical corporate bond-- tax-deductible, so it can be more competitive for them. So there are options for the issuers that are basically cheaper. So they're going with those, and they're calling back their prefs that, if they had reset them, would have cost them a lot in terms of the dollar amount of dividend payment and the implied coupon on a dividend rate on those prefs.

So that's the driver. And, like I said, it's been significant. We had, in the 2010s, many years of $5 billion or more a year of issuance. And we've seen the opposite in the last couple of years. There's been absolutely zero issuance for many years. And these redemptions have been running at many billions a year because of that substitution effect. And what it means is that buying spills into the remaining preferred shares in the market. That's a good thing for anybody who owns those securities. So it's been pushing prices higher.

Greg Bonnell: Pushing prices higher in the here and now. If this trend continues, where these big issuers-- traditional big issuers in preferreds-- start going to these other funding vehicles, what does the preferred market look like a year, five years, 10 years from now?

James Hunter: Well, it looks smaller, which is a double-edged sword. Because you want, ideally, a growing, liquid-- you'd call it healthy market-- but it's not as if really things have changed in the remaining parts of the market. The banks and insurance companies have really never-- it's almost weird to say it this way-- but they've never been doing better in terms of record corporate profits. The concerns about a recession and unemployment inching up notwithstanding, there are legitimate things to consider in that arena. But capital generation is really good. Margins are good. And the businesses are pretty steady right now. We've seen good dividend growth as well from the banks and insurance companies. So that part of the market is stable.

And then if you look at the pipeline, the utility companies, some of the other parts of preferred shares, we've got good growth projects, probably more than we've seen than in the last five or 10 years. If you think about power demand, that's a big theme in North America right now-- lots of natural gas projects as well. So that's an area where the utility companies can grow.

So, the remaining parts of the preferred share market are healthy. It's just that it may not be as big as it used to be in the past. And that's-- it can be frustrating from a liquidity perspective, but it's positive in terms of pricing because of the scarcity.

Greg Bonnell: OK. Quickly, I'm going to ask you, for someone who's just getting new to preferred shares, trying to figure out what role it could play in a portfolio, what role can it play?

James Hunter: Primary role is income. There are very, very few ways to get such a tax-advantaged yield. Now, often, we would look to corporate bonds for income. And that's absolutely correct. The thing is, is that you don't get a dividend tax credit on your corporate bond coupons. So, for a retail investor, those tax credits have a lot of value.

The other areas where you can get income would be in the income-oriented parts of the equity market. But yields have come down because the stock market has really roared off the bottoms in April. And dividend yields might be in the more of the 2-3% range on the TSX rather than 5%-plus in preferred shares. It's a very meaningful difference.

The other factor, of course, is GICs and cash in the last few years. You used to be able to get 4% or 5% on those short-term cash instruments. But, as the central banks in Canada have cut rates, those yields have come down. So preferred shares are this shining light where yields are still quite appealing and the dividend tax credits are there. So the role in a portfolio is income.

There is also supposed to be an element of diversification because it's a hybrid between stocks and bonds. And that's how we look at it. And in many of our income-oriented portfolios, it's a 5-10% weight. And that's what we're going for-- it's the income.

Original Post

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Tesla Skepticism, Google Stands Out, DORK Shorts
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Tesla's bad report (0:20). Google's outstanding report (2:50). Chipotle down after earnings (4:40). Return of the meme stocks (6:30). Big 4 reporting next week (12:50). Fed commentary coming (16:45).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, Seeking Alpha's Director of news, welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here. Thanks a lot.

RS: Our episode on Investing Experts yesterday was talking about waiting on tech earnings.

We've seen some of those come out, especially if you consider Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) a tech stock, company. Talk to us about what you're seeing. Is that top of mind for you right now, the earnings rolling in?

BS: Definitely. This was the first week of really blockbuster type earnings, after a little bit of a lead up last week.

Tesla, like you point out, is one of the the biggest names. Got a bit of a rebuke from shareholders. It's down about 9% after its earnings. Overall, bad report, but kind of expected that it would be bad.

Revenues down 12%. Unit sales down 14%. Net income down 23. The thing that I think is chipping away at the company's stock price today is the commentary from Elon Musk. He warned of a few rough quarters ahead.

I think a lot of people had hoped that Tesla had already kind of bottomed in terms of the political backlash and in terms of just the general core automotive business that might be returning to an upswing.

There are some signs that might not be the case, that investors should get ready for some bumpy waters ahead. Talk of tariffs, margin pressures, higher costs, just general economic worries along with the the problems that Tesla's faced so far this year.

On the other side of the spectrum, Musk aggressively popped up the longer term tech bets that they have, specifically robotaxis and the humanoid robots. He promised that autonomous ride hailing would be available to half of the US population by the end of this year.

I think that the fall in the stock price and some of the commentary afterwards suggests that there's some skepticism about that. Musk is pretty famous for making aggressive predictions and then just ignoring the fact that they don't come true.

So I think that there's a sense that maybe the auto business has deteriorated faster than people had feared, and the longer term bets are further off on the horizon than they had hoped.

And so there's going to be a period of time where there's gonna have to be a catch up for that.

RS: What other stocks would you add to the post earnings conversation?

BS: The biggest other stock to look at is Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL). As opposed to Tesla, had a a pretty outstanding report, beat expectations led by cloud growth. Cloud was up 32%, but there's also 12% growth in search.

There have been worries that the new AI competitors are gonna cut into Google search revenue, and that didn't happen. In fact, search was up pretty strongly. YouTube ads was also up 13%. That's a critical part of the business now too.

So all in all, strong report, the stock only up marginally after earnings was up about two percent earlier and is now just sort of hovering just above the flat line.

It had been drifting higher going into the earnings report. In fact, going into Wednesday, when the earnings came out, it was up 10 sessions in a row. So there was pretty good sense on Wall Street that strong numbers were coming. Google fulfilled expectations in that regard and is holding steady.

Longer term worries about valuation in the stock has already come up pretty strongly in the last few months.

The existential potential existential threat from AI is still hovering for the first time really since Google became a public company. I think there's a sign that its dominance in search might be at some point in the foreseeable future in doubt. And I think it's changed some of the thought around Google.

So I think that'll be a longer term conversation, obviously, but I think it's gonna be baked into every earnings report from now on - where is search sitting versus its AI competitors and what other businesses is Google ramping up to to take the load off of search as the key driver for the company.

RS: Outside of tech, what are you seeing? What stocks are at the top of your list right now?

BS: I think Chipotle (CMG) is an interesting one, down 13% after its earnings. It missed revenue estimates. Comparable store sales were down, which is rare for Chipotle.

Transactions were down about 4.9%. Margins were actually down a little bit as well. The company's seeing some inflation, but has offset a lot of that with higher prices and, efficiencies.

But I think that's just a worry in general, a popular retail sock like Chipotle, one that's very tied into the consumer, a little higher price in the overall fast food market, an upper scale fast food place.

So if you're seeing a few cracks forming there, I think there's concerns that that's tip of the iceberg in terms of a consumer that's starting to be more guarded about making expenditures.

RS: About their burrito choices.

BS: Exactly. Burrito budgets are shrinking.

RS: Inflation is hitting all components of the sectors.

T-Mobile (TMUS) saw some gains also. Are there other stocks that you would say had notable movements to add to that conversation? And what would you say about T Mobile's move?

BS: I think T Mobile's a good one to point out. Beat on new subscribers raises guidance. I think it's interesting because it's outside of the broader tech AI landscape.

It's not a sexy company. It's not one that growth investors might have on the radar, but it's interesting that it's just blocking and tackling getting new subscribers, churning higher.

Looking beyond the headline, it's interesting to peek under the hood for a company like that.

RS: What else do you have to say about the markets these days? Some volatility, some positivity, some skepticism. Where are your thoughts right now, or what are you seeing out of the marketplace?

BS: So I think, return of the meme stocks this week has been an interesting one, I didn't quite have that on my bingo card. They being the general market chatter has boiled down to the dork stocks.

So you have Krispy Kreme, (DNUT). You have Opendoor (OPEN), Rocket Mortgage (RKT), and Kohl's (KSS), so that's DORK.

But, honestly, it's a broader group of stocks. There's quite a few that are have seen surprising jumps higher. You have iRobot (IRBT), GoPro (GPRO), Red Robin (RRGB), Denny's (DENN), and this is just a a relatively short list of some of the stocks that have seen it. And the factor that is similar for all of these is just that they were heavily shorted.

So you're seeing short squeezes in these stocks that I would say have a name or are known by retail investors. The interpretations for this can be wide open.

Like why the meme stocks are back now. There could be an argument that's frothing the market, and maybe it's a negative sign, a bear sign that the market's reaching a top.

It might be a sign that a certain segment of the retail investor class is getting bored for lack of a better term, that the mind share of AI and tech stocks is shrinking, and so they're looking for other places for a pop.

If NVIDIA (NVDA) is not gonna pop 10% after earnings anymore, then there needs to be other ways to get that high. And so you might see that group of the investing segment looking for new ways to get those heavy one day jumps.

It might also be just one of those things, there's a class of retail investors who are meme stock devotees, and it's gonna come. It's almost like living on a fault line or living near a volcano. Every once in a while, it's gonna pop up. The only thing they really have in common is that they were heavily shorted.

RS: But why are they heavily shorted? Like, what catalyzed them into that group?

BS: It's a diverse group. I mean, you have iRobot. You have GoPro. So those are those are consumer facing tech companies. But you also have very mundane companies like Red Robin, Denny's, companies just going about their business.

And I think that the shorting aspect of it probably comes about because of worries about the consumer and worries that some of these stocks are either over leveraged, I mean, it's hard to talk about them as a group because each of the decisions might be very specific to that stock.

But I think if you're taking them as a whole, the idea that there were a lot of heavily shorted stocks to take advantage of in this light is probably a sign that there's another class of investors.

The sort of anti meme investors who are beginning to get a little skeptical about some of the areas of the market where things like higher costs inflation, things like a more cautious consumer, places where there are companies that have put themselves in a precarious position if those are the factors that are gonna take hold as we move forward.

RS: And forgive my banal question, but how it works is basically this is done on a large scale over a few investing firms?

BS: Yeah. So you have a ringleader who's able to identify these opportunities, and they will stir up a following online to buy these particular stocks that'll spark a short squeeze.

So there'll be programmatic trading at the firms that are shorting these stocks that if it gets above a certain level, it's gonna automatically cover the short.

And so as the the meme stock traders are trying to trip those wires, the covering of the short is a process of buying the stock so that sends it further higher. So it becomes a self fulfilling prophecy. Like, once you hit those auto triggers, you can send it higher.

It's sort of like a rock sitting on the edge of a cliff. If you can put enough sort of oomph into it, you can get it over the cliff, then gravity does the rest. The metaphor kind of breaks down because the stock's moving up, so the gravity seems to be pointing the other direction.

RS: Right before it moves down.

BS: Right. Well, there's that too. I mean, the key of any meme stock strategy is knowing when to sell. It can go up like we saw the first time that this was prevalent is it can go up a lot further than anyone could possibly guess.

Though in that first one, there were a lot of news reports of people who held on past ridiculous levels and ended up not making too much money off of a bet that should have made a ton.

So we'll see. It complicates the trading landscape in the sense that none of the people who are doing these meme stock pops that are leading that charge, they're not long term investors in the stock. They're not there for the three, five year time horizon.

So if you're an investor in Red Robin just to pick one at random or Krispy Kreme, it's a roller coaster, and you have to decide whether or not this is a lottery ticket that you were gifted, and now you just cashed in.

You got this 30% jump on a day, so I might as well get out of the stock now. Or if you're just gonna ride the ride because you've got a lot of hope in the company long term.

RS: That Investing Experts episode I mentioned where Jay Hatfield is talking about waiting on tech earnings. And by the way, he's bearish on Tesla. He was talking about the notion of getting in and out of these stocks, not just meme stocks, but obviously certainly pertains to that, is the difference between gambling and investing.

So interesting to see where your risk level is, what your stress tolerance is. All of those things are important to suss out before entering that fray.

We started the conversation with earnings. We have a slew coming up next week. What would you say investors should be on the lookout for?

BS: The big four next week are Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Apple (AAPL), and Amazon (AMZN). They all have a different story going on.

Just looking at Microsoft first, it's just off its fifty two week highs now. Recently reached them and drifted off of them. It's up 48% from its April lows. Positives going into the earnings report, Azure growth and partnership with OpenAI.

Its broader AI developments. So look for commentary about that coming out of the earnings. Concerns about Microsoft or valuation and just the spending that's necessary on the AI infrastructure.

And that's the theme that's gonna come up a lot is the process of building out these new AI products is expensive because you have to buy the infrastructure to get them going.

So the spending comes now and then hopefully the revenue later. And so in a lot of these cases, the question investors are asking is just what does that time horizon look like, that gap between spending and and payoff?

And I think that's the main theme from Meta. Meta's also up sharply since April, up 49% since the post first tariff concern drop off in April.

The main question around Meta is will the AI revenue catch up with spending? It's spending a lot to build that up. This is a habit with Meta.

Remember a few years ago when it went from Facebook to Meta, it was spending tons on the Metaverse, and the Metaverse as a popular topic has dropped off significantly since then, and there's been a pivot at Meta as there has been with many companies.

This isn't strictly a Meta thing towards an AI world. But that's just more spending that Meta has to do. So this is just another example of a build out that Meta has to do. And so investors were wondering, can they they start to get those investments to pay off?

If you're looking at Apple, it's along with Tesla, which obviously has its own problems, is sort of the the sick man of the the Mag seven. It's down 12% year to date.

Only Tesla among the Mag seven stocks is the only other one down, and Tesla's down sharper. But like I said, Tesla's facing the political ramifications of Elon Musk going to DOGE and then having a feud with the president and all these sorts of factors that are outside the business world, whereas Apple isn't facing anything on that level, but it's still down so far this year.

Positives have been continued growth of its services revenue so that's something to watch as earnings come out. On the negatives, it's had a very choppy AI rollout so investors will be looking for signs that that's starting to get and, again, it's a spending versus payoff situation.

Also, the valuation is high. And that's a concern for a lot of these stocks. There's been a pretty significant run. Apple, like I said, is kind of an exception.

But for your Microsofts of the world, there's been a significant run up this year. And so the question is whether that can sustain.

If we turn to Amazon, which is the last of the big four reporting next week, AWS growth is gonna be in focus. That's been a driver for a while now. So look for that unit and see how it's doing.

Meanwhile, on its core retail business, Amazon is open to issues with tariffs and just general competition in that area. So look for how well the company is navigating those headwinds.

RS: A lot of recent bullishness on Amazon as long as we're talking about it on investing experts. Three of the last four episodes, investors of all kinds are bullish on that stock. So if you're interested, check those out.

What would you say macro wise coming up next week or any other stocks to mention in the earnings conversation even briefly?

BS: We should just circle the economic stuff next week just because it's a pretty heavy week.

Earnings is obviously gonna be the main focus. We also have a Fed decision coming out. 97% chance currently baked in that there's not gonna be a cut. So this particular meeting outcome is probably gonna be pretty pretty boring.

But looking ahead to September, there's a 38% chance of a no cut, so about a two thirds chance that there's gonna be lower interest rates by the end of the September meeting.

So that's kind of being baked in to market expectations, the consensus of no cut this time. September, we're gonna start to cut. And then there's a better than 50% chance there'll be two cuts before the end of the year.

So I think at this meeting, look for commentary from Powell and the Fed in general to see where their head is at in terms of future cuts.

Hanging over all of this is the Trump versus Powell situation. Later today, Trump is scheduled to take a trip to the Fed headquarters.

There's been a lot of chatter about the cost of the refurbishment of the Fed, headquarters and whether or not some cost overruns there is gonna be used as an excuse to get rid of Powell.

Trump, of course, is looking for not just rate cuts, but extremely sharp rate cuts. He wants to drop it by three percentage points, which, we're talking about in September, we might get a quarter percentage point cut, and Trump wants 3% cut, three percentage points.

So the vision between the current Fed decision making and Trump's vision of what that should be couldn't be further enough away, really. So look for further commentary next week as that comes out.

I'm sure if there's a no cut decision, I'm sure Trump will have something to say about it. So I think that's gonna hang over that.

And then just in terms of the economic news, we have PCE on Thursday, so that's an inflation gauge. It's the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. That gives a good idea of where prices are at and then the jobs data Friday.

By the end of next week, we'll have had a Fed meeting. We'll have had an important inflation report, and we'll have had the jobs data. So you'll really be able to kind of triangulate what the economy is looking like and and what the Fed plans to do about it.

RS: By the way, file under the unprecedented happenings in 2025, that meeting that you mentioned with Trump going to the Fed headquarters, that's the first time it's happened in almost twenty years?

BS: The thing that it kinda reminded me - we'll see what happens - maybe it's not as many fireworks, but I don't know why you would do it if you were Trump unless you were trying to start the conversation again. But I had in mind the Zelensky meeting and sort of the talking down to, I I don't even know how to describe it. The calling to the principal's office vibe of that meeting, and whether or not there's gonna be a similar tone of Trump going to the Fed.

data.title: How The Gold-Silver Ratio Predicted Silver's Surge
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

Surges in the gold-to-silver ratio above 100 in both March 2020 and April 2025 preceded powerful silver rallies—and if the 2020 analog holds, even more silver gains are ahead.

I wanted to share an update highlighting a fascinating development—and the successful confirmation of a theory I proposed during the bleak days of early April.

That was when silver, along with most financial markets, plunged following President Trump’s surprising ‘Liberation Day’ tariff announcement. Silver sank a staggering 21% in just three trading sessions—a move that was both shocking and disheartening for precious metals investors.

Yet even then, I saw a glimmer of hope in the parallels with the early 2020 COVID lockdowns, which initially caused panic but ultimately sparked a powerful silver rally to seven-year highs. In this update, I’ll walk through how that theory played out—and what it still indicates for silver’s path ahead.

First, I want to show how silver recently broke out above its long-standing $32 to $35 resistance zone—a ceiling that held firm for over a year, from May 2024 to June 2025. This breakout marks, in my view, the official start of silver’s next major bull market. I believe silver is now on track to target $50 and even higher in the months ahead.

I also want to revisit gold briefly to point out how, from 2020 to 2024, it was capped below the $2,000 to $2,100 resistance zone. This ceiling kept gold suppressed for years until it finally broke out, igniting the powerful bull market it remains in today.

I see strong parallels between gold’s struggle then and silver’s recent battle under the $32 to $35 resistance zone. Now that silver has decisively broken out, there is a very strong chance it will follow in gold’s footsteps—and it’s even likely to outperform gold for the near future.

What’s remarkable—and encouraging—about this rally to 14-year highs is how unexpected it was, especially considering the dramatic selloff in early April when silver plunged 21% in just three trading sessions, falling from around $35 to as low as $27.54.

At the time, many pessimistic voices—including battle-weary and cynical silver investors—were convinced the metal was finished and headed back to $20 or even the teens. But the opposite happened—thankfully.

I have seen strong parallels between the tariff-induced plunge and the sharp 37% drop during the 2020 COVID lockdowns, when silver sank dramatically in just one month:

As a quick aside, it’s worth explaining why silver often sinks during periods of economic panic—despite its status as a precious metal.

Unlike gold, silver is far more tied to the industrial economy, with a much larger share of its demand coming from industrial use. That economic sensitivity can be both a blessing and a curse.

As the pie chart below shows, the majority of silver demand—about 55%—comes from industrial use, while only around 20% comes from investment.

In contrast, gold demand is driven largely by investment (44.57%) and jewelry (48.74%), with much of that jewelry serving as a form of investment as well, particularly in developing countries like India and China.

Returning to the main narrative, the weekly silver chart below highlights the striking parallels between silver’s 2020 and 2025 plunges—both followed by sharp recoveries to new highs:

Another key factor that tipped me off to a likely silver rebound after the April plunge was the behavior of the gold-to-silver ratio. In both 2020 and 2025, the ratio spiked above 100—an extreme level that signaled silver was deeply undervalued relative to gold.

For perspective, the long-term average since 1915 is 53, so a reading above 100 is a true anomaly. Such extremes have historically marked bullish turning points, and I saw it as a clear signal that silver was a screaming buy.

I likened the ratio to a rubber band stretched to its limit—unsustainable and primed to snap back. And sure enough, that’s exactly what happened.

Assuming the 2020 analog still holds, there is even more room for the ratio to decline, which would imply much higher silver prices ahead. That’s precisely what I expect.

As I mentioned earlier, the long-term gold-to-silver ratio dating back to 1915 averages 53, making the current reading of 86.3 still extremely elevated by historical standards.

I believe this ratio will fall significantly as silver’s bull market progresses.

Using some rough math, if gold remains at its current spot price of $3,367 and the ratio returns to its historical average of 53, that would imply a silver price of $63.52—a remarkable gain of about 63%. And that’s assuming gold doesn’t rise any further.

If gold continues to climb, as I expect it will, then the upside potential for silver grows even larger. I fully expect gold to reach at least $15,000 an ounce within the next five to ten years, driven by a severe global debt crisis and an eventual financial reset.

If the gold-to-silver ratio returns to its historical average of 53, that would imply a silver price of $283—an astonishing 625% gain from current levels.

In the shorter term, $4,000 gold is likely just around the corner, with both Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan forecasting that level by 2026—and I share that view.

Assuming that plays out, and the gold-to-silver ratio merely returns to 70 (where it was as recently as 2021), it would imply a silver price of $57—a solid and realistic gain of nearly 50%.

To wrap things up, the recent plunge in the gold-to-silver ratio has powerfully validated the theory I shared back in early April—that the ratio was stretched to an unsustainable extreme, like a rubber band ready to snap back.

And snap back it did, in a big way. If silver continues to track the 2020 analog, we should see the ratio drop even further and silver prices climb significantly higher. That would be a long-overdue reward for us patient, battle-tested silver investors—and will finally attract the broader attention and capital this undervalued asset deserves.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dorman Products: Exercising Caution As Earnings Approach
data.description:
One enterprise that has recently underperformed my expectations has been Dorman Products (NASDAQ:DORM). You see, whenever I rate a company a "Hold," I'm making the claim that shares should experience returns that would be similar to the broader market as measured by the S&P 500. In April 2025, I called the company a "Hold" candidate because of how the stock was priced. This was in spite of the fact that the overall financial picture for the business was improving. That improvement, while great to see, was not enough to justify an upgrade. But since then, the stock has risen by only 6.7%, significantly trailing the 19.7% increase achieved by the S&P 500. Looking at the picture again, I see that the stock is cheaper in at least one respect than it was at that time. But overall, my sentiments regarding the business are unchanged.

At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining the firm as a "Hold" candidate is quite sensible. However, the picture can change as new data comes in. After the closing bell on Aug. 4, management is expected to announce financial results covering the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue, earnings, and adjusted earnings will all come in higher year over year. But unless something really miraculous happens, I think that maintaining the current course makes the most sense.

No change in sentiment

In my previous article about Dorman Products, I was able to cover data through the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Results now extend through the first quarter of 2025. Because of this, my emphasis in this article will be on these most recent results. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $507.7 million. That represents an increase of 8.3% compared to the $468.7 million the business generated one year earlier. Management continues to boast that the number of distinct parts that the company offers to its customers is growing. In the quarterly report filed by the company, it stated that, at the end of last year, it marketed about 138,000 distinct parts to its customers. That was up from the 133,000 that it marketed the previous year. This doesn't even factor in private label SKUs and other variations in how the company markets, packages, and distributes its products.

This increase naturally helped the company. According to management, the rise in revenue was attributable not only to new products, specifically those in the Light Duty segment that the company has, but also to increased customer demand for goods in that segment. Unfortunately, there was some weakness elsewhere. The heavy-duty and specialty vehicle sectors, for instance, suffered from soft market conditions. It's a little difficult to know what's going on with the specialty vehicle space. But I'm not shocked at all when it comes to the heavy-duty side. Management mentioned in their annual report that lower freight industry shipping volumes, caused by broader economic weakness that stemmed from inflationary pressures and inventory destocking following a surge in inventory building during the COVID-19 pandemic, ultimately proved problematic for the space. Third-party sources have also shown that we have been in a freight recession for quite some time now.

The rise in revenue for the business brought with it higher profits as well. Net income shot up from $32.8 million to $57.5 million. The rise in sales helped, as did a drop in interest expense from $10.6 million to $7.4 million. But another very important driver here was a rise in the company's gross profit margin from 38.7% to 40.9%. Greater sales certainly help to create additional efficiencies. On top of this, management is working on certain productivity, automation, and supplier diversification initiatives aimed at creating cost savings for shareholders. The firm also benefited from a favorable mix of higher sales of new products. These have a tendency to bring with them higher margins.

Other profitability metrics for the firm have been largely positive. Adjusted net profits, for instance, grew from $40.8 million to $62.1 million. Operating cash flow dipped from $52 million to $51.2 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a surge from $50.1 million to $75.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $81.9 million to $108.5 million.

Management seems optimistic when it comes to 2025. They expect revenue to come in between 3% and 5% above what it was last year. That would indicate a slowing moving forward. And even though there are worries about economic conditions, they anticipate earnings per share of between $7 and $7.30. At the midpoint, this would be $220.2 million compared to the $190 million reported for last year. Hitting the midpoint of guidance for adjusted net profits would show an increase from $220.7 million to $237.2 million. Based on my own estimates, which draw on recent year-over-year performance, I would make the case that adjusted operating cash flow will likely come in at around $269.8 million, while EBITDA should come in at around $375.7 million. These would both represent increases over what the company saw last year.

If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. In the table below, I then compared the business to five similar enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. But when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach, this number shot up to four, while on an EV to EBITDA approach, it came in at three.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Dorman Products 15.6 13.7 10.0
Lear Corp (LEA) 12.4 5.7 5.4
LCI Industries (LCII) 16.0 5.9 8.9
Patrick Industries (PATK) 23.0 9.8 10.5
Modine Manufacturing (MOD) 27.4 23.7 15.1
BorgWarner (BWA) 28.4 5.0 8.3

At the start of this article, I also mentioned that management will be reporting financial results for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on Aug. 4. Leading up to that point, analysts anticipate sales of $517.1 million. This would represent an improvement over the $503 million the company reported for the second quarter of 2024. They also expect earnings per share to rise from $1.53 last year to $1.82, which would take net profits from $47.4 million to $56.1 million. Meanwhile, adjusted earnings per share are forecast to grow from $1.67 to $1.80. This would take the net profits up from $52 million to $55.4 million. In the table below, you can also see certain cash flow metrics for the second quarter of 2024. I would wager that most of these, if not all of them, will improve year over year.

It's important to note that there are some positives and negatives about the company. For starters, management is working on paying down debt. On top of investing $11 million in capital expenditures during the first quarter of this year, it successfully repaid $20 million of debt. At the same time, the business also repurchased about $12 million worth of stock. Considering how shares are priced, I would have much preferred that this capital be allocated toward additional debt reduction or growth initiatives. But things could be worse. Investors should also keep a careful eye out for any mentions of the tariff situation. In the first quarter earnings call for the company, management mentioned that there was considerable uncertainty here. They forecasted that weakened confidence levels in the economy would likely continue, but they also said that they anticipated that demand would return once the economy stabilizes.

There's a lot of uncertainty regarding tariffs right now, especially because between 30% and 40% of what the company buys comes from China. Interestingly, because of the FIFO inventory method that the company uses, it did not expect any impact on earnings for around six months. But investors will be able to get some idea of the pain by looking at cash flows. Of course, like any company significantly exposed to tariffs, management is working on mitigation efforts. This includes diversifying its supply chain, cutting costs, and imposing price increases on its customers. But with the tariff situation changing rather rapidly these days, nobody knows how this will work out.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that while Dorman Products remains an interesting business, it's not cheap enough, especially in light of broader economic concerns, for me to upgrade at this time. If we see some major improvements when management reports results early next month, my opinion on the matter could change. But for now, maintaining a "Hold" rating makes the most sense.

data.title: American Eagle: Solid Value And Awesome Turnaround Potential
data.description:
One of my favorite technical-trading bottom formulas put out a clear buy signal on American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (NYSE:AEO) yesterday, July 23rd, 2025. Retailing is a tough sector for investors, with changing fashions and consumer tastes plus a cutthroat competitive environment. Apparently, news that one of the most popular female acting stars in Hollywood, Sydney Sweeney, has jumped aboard the company's Fall back-to-school advertising campaign has caught Wall Street's attention. The business goal is to sell more high-dollar, high-profit margin jeans to youngsters. Well, the American Eagle homepage here is plastered with her picture in all types of jean products.

I am not a retail analyst. I don't know if the campaign will be a huge success or not. What I do know is that the AEO stock chart pattern is screaming buy to me. So, I have purchased a small starter position. Let me explain my logic for this.

Excellent Valuation Story

Shares are relatively cheap, getting an "A" Quant Factor Valuation Grade from Seeking Alpha's computer sorting system. The stock was trading just above book value, with a forecast P/E ratio under 10x next year's expected income result, before the latest announcement.

The forward 1-year earnings ratio of 9.6x is one of the lowest in the major apparel retailing group today. Interestingly, AEO has declined from one of the most expensive stocks on this financial metric to the opposite side of the spectrum for investors over the past 12 months.

When we include changing debt and cash positioning by the company, enterprise valuations are also close to bargain levels. EV to EBITDA around 4x and sales of 0.36x are in the bottom 25% and 5%, respectively, of long-term ranges over the last 20 years. In combination, only the early 2020 COVID pandemic shutdown of retail stores and the 2009 Great Recession generated lower valuations in recent memory.

At $11.30 per share, today's 9.9% free cash flow and 4.4% dividend yields don't hurt the investment appeal either. While neither is spectacular vs. other established retailers, both are well above average for the industry.

Short Squeeze Potential

The whole bullish argument is also helped by a large short interest position (a 30-month high in June), standing close to 13% of outstanding share counts, going into the news event. Sure, sales during 2025 to date have disappointed. Plus, the stock price has gone nowhere for a decade. Both have talked short sellers into confidently increasing their position size since early January, with total short interest doubling.

My investment take on the situation: we could see extensive short covering and Meme retail investor buying continue for weeks. I have witnessed more than a few huge stock gainers over the years after a celebrity name puts his/her weight behind a depressed stock. This could be a repeat bullish development.

Technical Chart Turnaround In July

Given shares have an impressive underpinning of value after a -60% price slide over the past 12 months into June's $9 price low, a sharp reversal higher cannot be ruled out. And, if the Sydney Sweeney news helps to spike company sales and profits unexpectedly, there is plenty of room for the stock quote to rebound 50%, maybe even 100%, back to last year's high level.

The first point to ponder on the technical trading chart is that the price has been trying to hold the April low level. American Eagle experienced major sales related to Wall Street fears of higher tariff costs on imported clothing (the business model). While the concern is definitely warranted, Asian inventory sourcing will be a headache for all competitors as well. The likely fix will be to pass along extra expenses through higher retail selling prices.

With the spike in price yesterday and today above its 50-day moving average, it appears something of a double bottom has been outlined in April and June. The good news is that regular and extensive selling volumes in the stock may have ended. Truly heavy volume days since early March (boxed in blue below) likely mean the average owner of AEO shares today is far different than when the new year began. It's possible strong-hand value buyers may not want to sell their shares until a retracement move plays out, with the price well above $11.

Other positives include accumulation patterns in the 20-day Chaikin Money Flow (circled in green) and 13-day Force Index (circled in purple) readings, in tandem during July, really for the first time in over a year.

Lastly, On Balance Volume has been zigzagging in the right direction since early June (boxed in gold), after a brutal decline from October. Taken together, I am quite bullish on the momentum pattern, confident enough that capitulation has taken place to reverse the price out of its downtrend. Followed and supported by months of basing in price, the next major move for American Eagle shares should be up. This assumes a stock market crash and/or slide into recession is not immediately ahead of us.

Another big reason I am buying AEO shares comes from my readout of the standard Renko chart setup. When we review the volume necessary to move price, heavy selling in June has been overcome in July (boxed in gold) with comparatively minor buying interest. To a degree, a vacuum of sellers may now exist. Translation: any new short covering and/or meme retail buying could push the price up quickly. The simple fact of improved sales trends at the operating business in the months ahead could also encourage extra share demand in the not-too-distant future. Such is the current supply/demand breakdown for the stock I like to watch closely.

Final Thoughts

Is American Eagle Outfitters a guaranteed winner? Absolutely not. My main operating business worry revolves around the decent odds of recession arriving in the second half of the year. However, customer excitement over Ms. Sweeney's promoted jeans may override a lackluster macroeconomic environment in the general retail outlook.

If sales do not rebound, it's entirely possible a serious recession sends the price under tangible book value, which is around $7 today. Yet, the odds of the stock staying below this level for more than several months look rather remote to me. The price-to-tangible BV multiple of 1.7x is already close to the lowest in trading history.

My thinking is that AEO's price around $10 may have already discounted the worst effects of Asian tariffs and a slowing U.S. economy (84% of FY2025 sales and 92% of long-lived assets in America). If this is true, not only could shares rise in price, but AEO could morph into a leading retail-industry investment performer through early 2026.

More good news for shareholders: management repurchased about 5% of outstanding ownership units, using cash on hand over the last quarter. This effort should leverage future price upside and "per share" results quite nicely for existing investors.

Given a "reversion-to-the-mean" investment outlook, a return trip to $15 or even $20 is possible over the next 6-12 months. Such would represent a normalized valuation zone on P/E, sales, cash flow, and book value, given only a minor blip higher for business results vs. past years.

In a best-case scenario, where surprising strength in sales is approaching, a price closer to $25 might be appropriate for a price target. A normalized valuation setup on a 10% or 15% jump in results over current expectations would get American Eagle to a double on your money (at $11.30 for a buy price) or more, when including the dividend payout over the next 12 months.

For sure, all of the above investment ingredients/variables deserve a Buy rating for American Eagle Outfitters. The upside reward potential appears to materially outweigh risks.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Betterware de Mexico Q2: Still Undervalued Despite A Strengthening Outlook
data.description:
Q2 results and full-year guidance for Betterware de México, S.A.P.I. de C.V. (BWMX) show a strong rebound for the business following a weak Q1, where revenue fell and margins declined, leading to negative FCF. The company returned to growth and FCF generation this quarter, with guidance pointing to an acceleration in revenue growth, coupled with margin expansion. Despite this, shares remain very cheaply valued, especially against relevant peers, offering a dividend yield of nearly 12%. I recommend staying long BWMX stock.

Key Takeaways From Q2 results

The company posted Q2 revenue and EBITDA, which were up 5.1% and 3.5% year over year, respectively. Amidst these results, the soft performance in the Betterware segment masks the strong performance from Jafra Mexico, where revenue and EBITDA growth were 10.9% and 14.2%, respectively, as shown below.

Jafra Mexico now contributes nearly 60% of the company's EBITDA, and its performance puts it ahead of U.S.-based beauty and personal care peers like L'Oreal (OTCPK:LRLCF) and Ulta Beauty (ULTA).

While I expect Jafra Mexico to be a key growth driver ahead, management appears to be making positive strides with respect to expanding to markets beyond Mexico, launching in Ecuador with a base of 2,500 associates. Though Ecuador's GDP is less than 7% of Mexico's, I believe the overall expansion plan which includes countries like Columbia and Peru, appears to be a significant growth opportunity.

Moving to FCF generation in Q2, it was up 29.2% year over year, reflecting a FCF conversion from adjusted EBITDA of 87.2%. This was mainly helped by inventory levels sequentially declining from Q1 by over 6%. Despite this, inventory levels were still 14.5% higher compared to Q2 2024, which implies that there is further room for inventory de-stocking, which should help lift FCF generation in the coming quarters.

Outlook And Risks To Consider

Management's latest guidance for the rest of the year implies that revenue growth is expected to accelerate to the low teens in the second half of FY25 versus the prior year period, with EBITDA expected to correspondingly increase by around 20%. While these expectations represent a sharp improvement compared to H1, it is notable that management is only assuming a stable macroeconomy, and not an improving one in their guidance, according to commentary on the call.

These improved growth expectations are founded on the strategic product introductions planned as well as the higher associate and distributor base at Betterware when exiting Q2. Additionally, Jafra Mexico continues to enjoy tailwinds from the Mexican beauty and personal care market that is forecasted to be growing at over a 5% CAGR for the coming years.

Turning to margins, a stronger Mexican Peso versus USD (MXN:USD) compared to H1 2025 should lead to lower import costs for goods. This, together with lower freight costs and less promotional activity, are what management attributes to expanding margins ahead.

When it comes to risks, it is important to acknowledge that demand for the company's products is sensitive to macroeconomic changes, a factor that we saw play out in Q1. Besides this, I believe the biggest risk for investors stems from the weak balance sheet, which is comprised of $243 million in net debt. In this regard, the strong FCF in Q2, along with expectations for continued strength in the second half, gives me confidence that leverage can decline over time from the current level where net debt-to-EBITDA stands at 1.97.

While geographic expansion into other Central American markets represents an attractive long-term growth opportunity, there is also the possibility that these expansions beyond Mexico are unsuccessful, much like the Betterware brand's earlier, unsuccessful attempt to enter the U.S. market. Nevertheless, I view the risk to be limited this time, given the much smaller scale of these markets and the lower capital requirements involved.

BWMX Stock Valuation

I expect FY25 FCF of $94 million, assuming a FCF conversion from EBITDA of 60%, at a currency exchange rate of 1 USD = 18.5 MXN. Based on BWMX's enterprise of $650 million, after accounting for its net debt of $243 million, shares are currently valued at an EV/FCF multiple of 6.9.

If I adjust for the temporary FCF headwinds in Q1 caused by higher taxes, normalized FCF conversion should be around 70%, which is close to historical levels, and implies a normalized FY25 FCF of $110 million. In this case, its EV/FCF valuation multiple drops to just 5.9, trading at just a fraction of the multiples assigned to relevant peers like L'Oreal and Ulta Beauty.

While a valuation discount may be warranted due to BWMX's concentration in the Mexican market and its reliance on the MLM model, I believe the current discount is too large and does not account for the business's positive aspects, such as its capital-light nature and diversification between the Betterware and Jafra brands.

Meanwhile, as investors await a potential multiple re-rating higher, they stand to benefit from the stock's massive FCF yield on equity, which stands at over 22%, while offering a dividend yield of nearly 12%.

BWMX Stock Remains A Buy

The performance for Jafra Mexico was particularly impressive in Q2, and the company's overall growth story remains intact, especially with the geographic expansion ahead. Margins remain healthy and are set to improve in the coming quarters, enabling strong FCF generation, which in turn should alleviate debt concerns. The cheap valuation and attractive dividend yield make BWMX stock still a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Paramount's Legacy Business Shrinks Faster Than Streaming Grows
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Paramount (NASDAQ:PARA) (NASDAQ:PARAA) is a stagnating company despite a growing streaming business. However, this is not growing fast enough to compensate for the declining business sector, which means that sales have tended to stagnate or fall for years, and content generation for streaming is eating up a lot of money. I just don't see enough evidence that the business will improve significantly, and given the stagnation, I don't think the valuation is that favorable.

This article will not talk about which new content they produce or the Skydance merger, as I think it was discussed enough in other articles. It is also not discussing a potential arbitrage play, which Seeking Alpha author Paul Franke did here very well. I want to focus on the big picture and the raw numbers. Unless otherwise stated, my articles are more focused on whether it is an attractive long-term investment. And as far as that is concerned, I have not built up enough confidence in Paramount after my analysis.

Let's start with the long-term chart.

It looks like a company in decline. The question is whether Paramount Global can make a successful turnaround. It almost seems like a race: Can the rise of the new streaming segment, Paramount+, make up for the decline of the legacy TV media segment? I'll try to find out in this article.

Industry Overview

Streaming is becoming more competitive as more companies are trying to enter this market. A few days ago, "Nielsen's Media Distributor Gauge" was released, where YouTube leads for the fifth time in a row and even continues to extend its lead, while Netflix records the biggest gain and enters the top 3 for the first time.

The Gauge™ is Nielsen's monthly snapshot of total broadcast, cable and streaming consumption that occurs through a television screen, providing the industry with a holistic look at what audiences are watching. (...)

while time spent watching streaming content in June was up over 5% compared to May, Netflix viewership surged 13.5% over the same period. (...)

YouTube also received a boost from school-aged audiences in June and remained atop the Media Distributor Gauge rankings for a fifth consecutive month. The streamer notched a 6% monthly viewing increase over May and represented 12.8% of TV usage, its largest share of television to date.

nielsen.com

To me, this is quite crazy: it is very expensive for companies like Paramount to enter the streaming market and produce the necessary content. Whereas Google spends nothing to produce content for YouTube, the users do it themselves, and just because they get a share of the revenue. And yet YouTube is much more popular overall.

Overall, the growth of subscription or ad-based video streaming seems far from over, and the US market is by far the biggest.

The streaming video revolution is still in full flower — with the sector poised for healthy growth over the next five years, according to the latest projections from PwC.

The U.S. total over-the-top (OTT) market is projected to increase at a 5.9% compound annual growth rate over the next five years — reaching $112.7 billion by 2029, according to this year's PwC Global Entertainment & Media Outlook report. That would be up 33% from the segment's $84.7 billion haul last year. The primary drivers of that are a growing subscriber base, new service launches and price increases, according to PwC's analysis.

variety.com

The Past: Financial Progress and Trends

First, an overview over a longer period for revenues, expenses, and free cash flow. I think it makes sense to look at this at the beginning to understand the big picture of where the company roughly stands. What we can see is stagnating revenue and free cash flow, which is only marginally positive and hasn't always been in every quarter.

In the chart above, it looks like the margin was much better around 2021. Below we see that margins were indeed stronger in 2021 and have mostly been on a downward trend since then. One exception is the improvement in gross profit margin from 2024, but this is not the most important figure, as it was improved by layoffs.

I created this income statement comparison for the last quarter compared to the previous year, showing where money is spent and how sales and costs are generally developing.

So, overall, pretty bad numbers, in my opinion. The most worrying thing for me is that revenue is down 6% while the cost of revenue is down much less in percentage terms. This has led to significantly worse gross profit and operating income. Net income has no year-on-year comparison as it was negative $5.4B in the previous year due to special effects.

Overall, I think the dividend is a good measure of the financial health of the company. A constant or rising amount of free cash would probably lead to stable or increasing dividends, but this is not the case. I also added the total dividends paid in dollars; you can see that since 2023 there is less and less money available for the dividend. That's okay, insofar as the health of the business is of course the top priority, but it doesn't paint a good picture in terms of business development. At least for me, when investing in a barely growing value company, I would like to have secure and preferably high dividends.

Paramount+

As I wrote at the beginning, it's like a race overall, where the rising revenue from the streaming division has to make up for the falling revenue from the TV Media division. But it's not happening; as a result, the company's total revenue is not increasing.

Looking closer at these numbers above and also the latest quarterly results per segment, we can see that the shrinking of TV Media happens faster than the rise of Direct-to-Consumer.

The company additionally explains:

Total company revenue decreased 6%, which included an 8 percentage point impact from CBS's broadcast of Super Bowl LVIII in the year ago period. Excluding the Super Bowl comparison, Q1 revenue grew 2%. Total company advertising revenue decreased 19%, reflecting a 19 percentage point impact from CBS's broadcast of Super Bowl LVIII in the year ago period. Excluding the comparison, Q1 advertising revenue was unchanged.

Q1 2025 results

But in my opinion, these explanations are not that important. In every research, I find an explanation for poor results. And in the next quarter, there are other explanations. In the charts above, we see a long-term decline in the TV Media segment, and the last quarter followed this trend; these are the hard facts that are more important than the explanations.

The Present: Valuation and Current Developments

The company is currently valued at an enterprise value of $22.5B; its market cap is $9.3B, which means there is net debt of around $13B.

In terms of valuation, we can look at different approaches and compare them with other companies. It is fair to say that the stock is relatively cheap-by far the cheapest in terms of forward P/E and EV/EBITDA. I think EBITDA is a useful metric for comparisons to show the ability to generate cash before taking into account interest payments or taxes. But Paramount is also the slowest-growing among these four.

A 5-year chart of the forward P/E ratio of the company separately, showing that it used to be even cheaper.

The Future: Outlook

What to expect looking forward? Overall, it is very hard to judge because it seems like the company is quite dependent on further rising subscriber numbers of its streaming segment, as the TV media segment will probably continue to decline; at least this is the long-term trend.

But like I mentioned earlier, the streaming segment is increasingly crowded, and the more existing subscribers, the more difficult it gets to grow this number even further. Especially when YouTube is still growing in popularity and Netflix is outspending Paramount by a wide margin.

Paramount+ revenue grew 33% in the year, 10 million new subscribers came to Paramount+ for a total of 77.5 million, and DTC profitability improved by nearly $1.2 billion. More importantly, more than a quarter of the company's revenue is now generated in our DTC segment, which positions us well for the future. And, with continued focus on strengthening our financial position, we also executed our stated goal of $500 million in annual run-rate cost savings.

June 2025 letter to stockholders

What we can do is to create a few different scenarios for possible future share prices. Let's take the estimates as a basis and use three different P/E ratios.

Year EPS estimate P/E 7 (Pessimistic) P/E 10 (base case) P/E 13 (optimistic)
2025 $1.26 $8.82 $12.60 $16.38
2026 $1.45 $10.15 $14.50 $18.85
2027 $1.43 $10.01 $14.30 $18.59

As I'm writing, the stock price is $13.26. So, assuming the EPS estimates will be accurate, the only attractive scenarios are those with higher P/E ratios, such as 13 in this case. If it remains at around 10, as it is now, the stock would remain relatively unchanged. An even lower P/E ratio would result in a heavy loss. To me, it looks like a stock owner would be quite dependent on a multiple expansion to make money, which is not an attractive scenario to be in.

It should also be noted that in the past, earnings revisions have tended to go down; so, analysts may gradually lower their estimates further, which will make the expected return even worse than in my table above.

All in all, these various scenarios seem rather unattractive to me, considering that although one segment of the company is growing, another is in slow decline. Overall, it is a stagnant company.

AI - Risk or Opportunity?

A big elephant in the room is the emergence of AI video, and I'm honestly not sure whether this is more of an opportunity or a risk for companies like Paramount, Disney (DIS), and Netflix (NFLX). On the one hand, they already have the brands and the user base. Their best-case scenario is to use AI video generation to greatly reduce costs and improve margins.

On the other hand, they don't own the AI models, so the same models would also enable numerous new players to enter the market. Up to now, there have been high entry barriers due to high costs. But if the production of films and series becomes considerably cheaper thanks to AI video, we can assume that numerous other competitors will enter the market in the future.

Overall, I expect a flood of new content in the future, which will mean that only the best of the best content will prevail. It remains to be seen to what extent all this will affect today's media companies. However, I believe there is a lot of potential for disruption from small, more flexible companies.

Share Dilution, Insider Trades & SBCs

Overall, all three aspects are fine: the outstanding share count is rising only slowly, SBCs are rather low, and I have not found any insider trades for the last six months.

Conclusion

Overall, I'm not convinced. The still largest core business is constantly shrinking, while the company is trying to gain a foothold in a competitive new segment where Netflix spends considerably more money on content. On the various charts, we saw that the shrinking is happening faster than the growth. In my view, there is simply too little tangible to suggest a positive share performance over the next few years (leaving aside the potential arbitrage play). I therefore rate the share as a sell, because that's what it is for me in the long term.
